flashcards.front: Why does a do-while loop guarantee at least one execution while a while loop may execute zero times?
flashcards.back: A do-while checks its condition AFTER executing the loop body, so the body runs once before any condition check. A while loop checks its condition BEFORE execution, so if false initially, the body never executes.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between 'break' and 'continue' statements in C++ loops?
flashcards.back: break terminates the entire loop immediately, transferring control to the statement after the loop. continue skips the remaining statements in the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration check.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of nested loops where the outer loop runs n times and the inner loop runs m times?
flashcards.back: O(n × m). Each iteration of the outer loop triggers m inner loop iterations, resulting in n*m total executions. For nested loops with the same variable (i.e., i from 0 to n), complexity is O(n²).

flashcards.front: When should you prefer a switch statement over if-else chains in C++?
flashcards.back: Use switch when comparing a single variable against multiple constant integral or enumeration values. It's more efficient (single jump table lookup) than multiple if-else evaluations. Avoid switch for complex conditions, ranges, or non-constant comparisons.

flashcards.front: What is an off-by-one error in loop iterations, and how does it occur in C++?
flashcards.back: An off-by-one error occurs when a loop iterates one too many or one too few times. Common causes: using <= instead of < (or vice versa), incorrectly initializing loop counters, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing in C++ arrays.

flashcards.front: How can an infinite loop be created accidentally in C++, and how can you detect and prevent it?
flashcards.back: Infinite loops occur when the loop condition never becomes false: forgetting to update the loop variable, using floating-point numbers where precision causes the condition to never trigger, or writing while(true) without break. Detect via debugger breakpoints; prevent by ensuring loop variable updates and using bounded conditions.

flashcards.front: Compare the performance of for, while, and do-while loops in C++ for equivalent iterations.
flashcards.back: Performance is identical when compiled—the compiler generates equivalent machine code for equivalent logic. Choice depends on readability: use for when iteration count is known, while when condition-based, do-when at least one iteration is guaranteed.

flashcards.front: What is loop unrolling, and why might a compiler perform it?
flashcards.back: Loop unrolling replicates the loop body multiple times per iteration to reduce loop overhead (condition checking and branch instructions). A compiler may unroll small, fixed-size loops to improve performance at the cost of increased code size.
